content.text: Async code fails differently from synchronous code. A thrown exception inside a callback or a rejected Promise does not unwind the call stack the way a synchronous throw does — unless you explicitly handle it. Understanding where errors surface, how they propagate through
chains, and what happens when they go unhandled is essential for writing reliable React applications.
Why async error handling is different
In synchronous code, an uncaught exception bubbles up the call stack until it reaches a
block or crashes the program immediately. With Promises, rejection is a separate channel — a rejected Promise that has no
handler produces an unhandled rejection warning in the browser and can crash a Node.js process.
syntax bridges this gap: a rejection from an awaited Promise is re-thrown as a synchronous exception at the
works. But there are still several ways errors can silently escape.
The diagram below traces what happens when an awaited function rejects at different levels of the call chain.

Error propagation through the call chain
function throws (or re-throws), its returned Promise rejects. Any caller that
s it receives that rejection as a thrown exception at the
point. This means errors naturally propagate up the chain — each level can either handle or re-throw.
The pattern works like synchronous exception propagation, but through the Promise chain. Each
is a potential throw site; each
block receives any thrown value. Use
checks or custom error classes to distinguish error types and respond accordingly.

returns a new Promise. If the callback inside it does not throw, the outer Promise resolves with the return value — which can mask errors if you are not careful.
When running multiple async operations in parallel with
, any single rejection causes the entire group to reject. Use
instead when you want all results — fulfilled and rejected — and need to process them individually.
If a rejected Promise has no
, it becomes an unhandled rejection. Both environments provide a global hook.
In older Node versions, unhandled rejections printed a warning. Since Node 15, the default behaviour is to crash the process with a non-zero exit code — the same as an uncaught synchronous exception.
block exits before the Promise settles — the rejection escapes entirely.
block hides failures. At minimum, log the error or rethrow it so higher-level handlers can respond.

flag prevents state updates after the component unmounts — a common source of React warnings. The
state carries the message to the UI rather than letting the error disappear silently.
A small helper can eliminate repetitive
blocks when you want to handle errors inline without try/catch syntax.
This style makes the error path explicit at every await site without nesting
blocks. Whether to use it is a matter of team preference.
block runs. It is ideal for cleanup such as hiding a loading spinner, closing a connection, or releasing a resource.
When to catch vs. when to re-throw
A useful mental model: handle the error at the level that has enough context to respond meaningfully.
when you can recover or provide a meaningful fallback (empty list, cached value, default state).
when the current layer cannot decide what to do — let the caller decide.
when you want observability at an intermediate layer but still want higher-level handlers to decide the response.
when you want to add context to a low-level error before it surfaces to the user.
— it lets you attach the original error to the wrapper, which is invaluable for debugging.

Never swallow errors silently; always log, surface to state, or re-throw.
